Age Verification via EUDI Wallet: Technical Risks, Societal Consequences, and Alternative Regulatory Approaches

A Critical Analysis of the Proposed Mandatory Online Identification

Compiled from academic sources, court rulings, and official EU documents. March 2026.

How This Analysis Was Created

This analysis was generated using Claude (Anthropic), a large language model. The author provided the research question and iteratively refined the output through structured prompts covering technical architecture, legal frameworks, societal implications, and regulatory alternatives.

All referenced sources — academic papers, EU regulations, court rulings, and institutional reports — were independently verified by the author. The AI served as a research accelerator and drafting tool; editorial judgment, source validation, and final argumentation remain with the human author.

Tools used: Claude (Anthropic) for research synthesis and drafting • Manual source verification • Claude Code for formatting and publication.

Abstract

In February 2026, senior SPD politicians published a policy paper calling for a social media ban for children under 14, a “youth version” for 14–15-year-olds, and comprehensive age verification via EUDI Wallet — including for adults. Using this proposal as a policy trigger, this analysis examines EUDI-based age verification broadly. It demonstrates that the EUDI Wallet architecture (ARF v1.4/1.5) fails to deliver the privacy-preserving age verification that proponents promise, and conducts a structured proportionality analysis under Art. 52(1) EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. The analysis finds that universal EUDI-based verification fails the necessity and proportionality stricto sensu tests: less invasive alternatives — privacy-preserving age assurance, design regulation (UK AADC), DSA enforcement — achieve the legitimate child protection objective without creating permanent mass identity infrastructure. The paper advocates a pro-better-technology position: privacy-preserving age assurance combined with robust design regulation that targets the surveillance advertising business model driving online harms, rather than the identity of users.

Keywords: age verification, EUDI Wallet, platform governance, proportionality analysis, surveillance, chilling effects, design regulation, digital identity, contextual integrity, fundamental rights

Theoretical Framework & Method ‹

Theoretical Framework

This analysis draws on three intersecting frameworks:

First, Contextual Integrity (Nissenbaum, 2010): Age verification via EUDI Wallet transmits identity data from one informational context (government identification) to another (social media use), violating the context-relative informational norms governing both. The “function creep” documented in Section 2.4 represents systematic context collapse.

Nissenbaum, H. (2010). Privacy in Context: Technology, Policy, and the Integrity of Social Life. Stanford University Press.

Second, Platform Governance Theory (Gillespie, 2018; Van Dijck et al., 2018; Helberger et al., 2018): Platforms are not neutral intermediaries but actively shape public discourse through design choices. Age verification regulates who accesses the platform; design regulation changes what the platform does — a distinction that maps onto Lessig’s (2006) four modalities of regulation (law, norms, market, architecture/code).27

Gillespie, T. (2018). Custodians of the Internet: Platforms, Content Moderation, and the Hidden Decisions That Shape Social Media. Yale University Press.

Van Dijck, J., Poell, T. & de Waal, M. (2018). The Platform Society: Public Values in a Connective World. Oxford University Press.

Helberger, N., Pierson, J. & Poell, T. (2018). “Governing online platforms: From contested to cooperative responsibility.” The Information Society, 34(1), 1–14. DOI: 10.1080/01972243.2017.1391913.

Lessig, L. (2006). Code: Version 2.0. Basic Books.

Third, Surveillance Studies (Lyon, 2018; Zuboff, 2019): Mandatory identification normalizes surveillance as a condition for participation in digital public life. Lyon’s concept of “surveillance culture” — where surveillance becomes an accepted, even participatory, practice — describes the risk that age verification habituates citizens to presenting identity credentials as a routine precondition for online activity.28

Lyon, D. (2018). The Culture of Surveillance: Watching as a Way of Life. Polity Press.

Method

This paper employs a critical policy analysis methodology (cf. Fischer, 2003) combining: (1) technical analysis of the EUDI Architecture Reference Framework, drawing on published cryptographic assessments; (2) comparative case analysis of prior identification mandates (South Korea, EU COVID Certificate, India’s Aadhaar); (3) fundamental rights assessment under EU, ECHR, and German constitutional law; and (4) regulatory alternatives assessment.29

Limitations: The analysis does not conduct original empirical research or independent cryptographic audit. Case studies were selected for their relevance and documentation quality, which may introduce selection bias toward negative outcomes — the comparison class is weighted toward authoritarian regimes (China, Turkey, Russia) and negative precedents, while democratic states with largely successful digital identity deployments (Estonia, Denmark, Sweden, Finland) receive less attention (though Part 2b addresses this partially). The regulatory landscape is rapidly evolving (the Digital Fairness Act has not yet been proposed; French age verification pilot results are not yet available). The paper’s normative position — that mandatory age verification is disproportionate — is stated upfront and present in the abstract; this is a critical policy analysis with an argumentative thesis, not a neutral survey. Readers should evaluate the evidence independently.

Additional limitations acknowledged since v2.1: (1) The analysis does not systematically examine the political economy of the age verification debate — specifically, which industry actors benefit from identity-based regulation versus design regulation, and how lobbying dynamics shape the policy discourse. (2) Positions of key institutional actors beyond those cited — notably ENISA (EU Agency for Cybersecurity) on identity infrastructure risks and the BfDI (German Federal Data Protection Commissioner) on EUDI Wallet implementation — are not systematically integrated. (3) The paper does not propose falsifiable success metrics for either age verification or design regulation, making it difficult to evaluate either approach empirically after implementation. (4) The CJEU’s ruling in C-252/21 (Meta Platforms, 04.07.2023) — which clarified the interaction between GDPR and competition law regarding data processing — is not integrated into the analysis of platform data practices, though it may be relevant to the enforcement discussion in Section 3.3.

Methodological note on Fischer (2003): This paper applies Fischer’s first three levels of evaluation (technical-analytical verification, contextual validation, societal-level vindication) but does not systematically perform the fourth level (ideological discourse analysis) — i.e., an explicit interrogation of whose discursive frames dominate the debate and why “child protection” rather than “platform accountability” or “surveillance normalization” has become the primary framing. This is a limitation the reader should bear in mind.

Fischer, F. (2003). Reframing Public Policy: Discursive Politics and Deliberative Practices. Oxford University Press.

Part 0: The SPD Proposal — Policy Trigger and Analytical Scope ‹

In February 2026, senior SPD politicians — including the party’s digital policy spokesperson — published a policy paper proposing a comprehensive framework for social media age verification. The proposal contains three core elements:74

  1. Under-14 ban: Children under 14 should be prohibited from using social media platforms entirely.
  2. Youth version (14–15): Adolescents aged 14–15 should access a restricted “youth version” of social media platforms, with reduced algorithmic personalization and content restrictions.
  3. Universal age verification via EUDI Wallet: All users — including adults — must verify their age using the European Digital Identity Wallet. The proposal explicitly names the EUDI Wallet as the technical mechanism.

The proposal’s scope is expansive: it applies to all social media platforms, not merely to services hosting age-restricted content (pornography, gambling). Enforcement would operate through platform liability — platforms that fail to implement age verification face regulatory consequences under national and EU law.

What the SPD Proposal Does Not Specify

The policy paper leaves critical implementation questions unanswered. It does not specify: (1) the technical mechanism by which the EUDI Wallet would transmit age attestations — whether via full credential presentation, selective disclosure, or zero-knowledge proof; (2) how the “youth version” for 14–15-year-olds would differ technically from the adult version, and who would define and audit the content restrictions; (3) what data retention obligations would apply to platforms receiving age attestations; (4) how enforcement would operate across jurisdictions for platforms headquartered outside the EU; or (5) what oversight mechanisms would prevent function creep beyond age verification. These gaps are not mere implementation details — they determine whether the proposal can satisfy the proportionality requirements analyzed in the Interim section.

The European Context

The SPD proposal does not exist in isolation. France enacted the Loi SREN (2024) requiring age verification for pornographic websites. Australia legislated a minimum age of 16 for social media in November 2024. The UK’s Online Safety Act (2023) combines design regulation with age assurance requirements. Spain announced plans for a state-developed age verification app in 2024. The European Commission’s expected Digital Fairness Act (Q3 2026) will address addictive design and special protection for minors. These parallel developments reflect a shared policy impulse — but they adopt fundamentally different technical approaches, ranging from age estimation (Australia) to architectural role separation (France) to technology-neutral frameworks (UK). The SPD proposal is distinctive in its reliance on a specific EU infrastructure (the EUDI Wallet), which ties a national child protection policy to a continent-wide identity system.

Analytical Reframing

This analysis uses the SPD proposal as its policy trigger but examines EUDI-based age verification more broadly. The SPD paper is representative of a wider European trend, and the technical architecture (the EUDI Wallet) is an EU-wide infrastructure whose implications extend beyond any single national proposal.

A critical reframing is necessary regarding the EUDI Wallet itself. The Wallet is mandated by Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0) regardless of whether it is used for age verification. Member states must offer EUDI Wallets to all citizens by 2026. The infrastructure will exist whether or not the SPD proposal is adopted. The danger identified in this analysis is therefore not the creation of the EUDI Wallet — that decision has been made — but the normalization of identity credentials for content access. Each new use case that requires presenting a Wallet credential lowers the political and psychological threshold for the next. Age verification for social media transforms a voluntary government identity tool into a de facto mandatory precondition for participation in digital public life.75

The analysis proceeds as follows: Part 1 examines the technical architecture of the EUDI Wallet and its privacy deficiencies. Part 2 documents the societal and fundamental rights implications of mandatory digital identification. Part 2b presents the strongest case for age verification. The Interim section conducts a structured proportionality analysis under Art. 52(1) EU Charter. Part 3 analyzes the root causes of online harms and evaluates alternative regulatory approaches. The Conclusion synthesizes findings and states the paper’s position.

Part 1: Technical Analysis of the EUDI Wallet Architecture ‹

1.1 EUDI Wallet — Pseudonymous, Not Anonymous

Note on ARF versioning: This analysis is based on the Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) version 1.4 (published February 2024). ARF v1.5 was released in December 2024. While v1.5 introduces editorial clarifications and expanded implementation guidance, it does not resolve the core privacy deficiencies identified below: the credential format remains SD-JWT/mdoc with static issuer signatures; BBS-based unlinkable proofs remain optional, not mandatory; the Pseudonym Provider architecture is retained; and wallet instance attestation continues to function as a potential linking vector. The cryptographers’ joint statement (June 2024) and the epicenter.works analysis (2024) were directed at v1.4 but their structural criticisms remain applicable to v1.5. Where v1.5 introduces substantive changes relevant to specific points, these are noted inline.71

The SPD calls for age verification via EUDI Wallet and promises “no restriction of anonymity.” The technical reality fundamentally contradicts this promise.

The Problem: Signed Credentials

Germany relies on the so-called signed credentials model: identity data is cryptographically signed — i.e., digitally certified — by a government authority and then transmitted to the respective recipient. Since the signature remains valid beyond the original transaction context (as long as the credential is unrevoked and the cryptographic primitives hold), any recipient can pass the signed data on to third parties unchanged — it remains verifiable without the holder being able to detect or prevent this. Cryptographers and data protection authorities have explicitly warned against this model.

“An issue with any digital credential is that it can easily be copied and thus illegally distributed.”

— Slamanig, D. (2025). “Privacy-Preserving Authentication: Theory vs. Practice.”1

Unlinkability (Nichtverkettbarkeit) Is Not Guaranteed in the Current Design

Both data formats specified in the Architecture Reference Framework (ARF, version 1.4, the version analyzed throughout this paper unless otherwise noted) — SD-JWT and ISO 18013-5/mdoc — use static, issuer-generated digital signatures over the (hashed) identity attributes of the user. This signature functions as a persistent identifier — it remains identical with each presentation. Any two service providers (so-called Relying Parties) can therefore link transactions of the same user with minimal effort.

“the signature as well as (h1,...,hn) are always revealed and thus can be trivially linked by any RP.”

— Slamanig, D. (2025).1 Keynote, 19th IFIP Summer School on Privacy and Identity Management.

Joint Statement by International Cryptographers

In June 2024, 16 internationally renowned cryptographers published a joint statement documenting the fundamental deficiencies of the ARF.2 Signatories include, among others:

  • Anna Lysyanskaya (Brown University) — co-inventor of CL signatures (Camenisch-Lysyanskaya, CRYPTO 2001/2004)
  • Jan Camenisch (Dfinity) — co-inventor of CL signatures, IBM Idemix
  • Bart Preneel (KU Leuven) — one of the most cited cryptographers worldwide
  • Carmela Troncoso (EPFL) — led the DP-3T protocol for COVID contact tracing
  • Anja Lehmann (Hasso Plattner Institute, University of Potsdam)
  • Daniel Slamanig (Universität der Bundeswehr München)

Their core criticism: The technology to ensure anonymity has existed for over 20 years (BBS signatures, CL signatures, U-Prove). The ARF architects deliberately chose against it. BBS (as being standardized by IRTF CFRG) enables unlinkable proof of knowledge of a valid signature — the holder derives a cryptographically fresh, unlinkable zero-knowledge proof per presentation rather than revealing the issuer’s static signature — combined with zero-knowledge selective disclosure. The current ARF architecture cannot do this. Notably, U-Prove (Microsoft Research) operates on standard elliptic curves (including P-256) without requiring pairings, demonstrating that anonymous credentials are not inherently incompatible with existing hardware — though U-Prove offers more limited selective disclosure than BBS and its development has been largely discontinued.59

Batch Issuance: A Proposed but Insufficient Workaround

The ARF acknowledges the linkability problem and proposes batch issuance as a mitigation: the issuer provides the holder with multiple copies of the same credential, each bearing a distinct signature. The holder uses a different copy per presentation, preventing cross-verifier correlation. However, cryptographers consider this an inadequate substitute for proper unlinkable proof schemes: the number of unlinkable presentations is bounded by batch size, re-issuance creates new tracking vectors, and revocation checking can re-link batched credentials — specifically, if each credential in a batch has a unique index in the OAuth Status List (draft-ietf-oauth-status-list), the revocation infrastructure can correlate presentations across verifiers by observing which index is checked, completely defeating batch issuance’s unlinkability goal. Privacy-preserving revocation mechanisms exist (e.g., accumulator-based revocation as in CL credentials) but were not adopted. Notably, several EU-funded research projects (OLYMPUS, IMPULSE) have developed and tested privacy-preserving credential systems — yet their results were not integrated into the ARF.30

Additional Hardware Constraint: BBS signatures require pairing-friendly elliptic curves (primarily BLS12-381, as specified in the IETF draft). Mass-market secure elements in smartphones lack support for pairing-based cryptography — while they support curves beyond P-256 (including P-384, P-521, and in some cases Ed25519), none support the pairing operations BBS requires. The bottleneck is the pairing computation itself, not merely curve support. However, hybrid approaches that delegate pairing operations to software while using the secure element for key storage are being actively developed (cf. IACR ePrint 2025/619, “Making BBS Anonymous Credentials eIDAS 2.0 Compliant,” already cited in this paper’s references but not incorporated into the ARF).3

Wallet Instance Attestation as a Further Linking Vector: The ARF requires wallet instances to be attested — proving they are genuine, unmodified wallet apps running on secure hardware. This attestation can itself function as a persistent identifier if not carefully designed, providing wallet providers and OS platform vendors (Google, Apple) with an additional mechanism to correlate transactions across verifiers.

1.2 Zero-Knowledge Proofs Are Not Mandatory

Zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP) are mentioned in the eIDAS 2.0 Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183) only in the recitals — not as a binding requirement in the implementing acts. The European Commission has systematically ignored the relevant data protection provisions in the technical implementing specifications.

“The ARF simply ignores this paragraph completely.”

— epicenter.works (2024). Data Protection Analysis: eIDAS Architecture Reference Framework 1.4.4

1.3 Lack of Protection Against Tracking by Wallet Providers

The ARF (v1.4) does not mandate an architecture that prevents wallet providers — or the underlying OS platform (Android/iOS) — from observing transaction metadata. Several implementation paths would expose transactions: cloud-based key storage, wallet instance attestation requiring backend communication, and OS-level telemetry that can observe wallet activity independently of the wallet app itself.

“Every use of the wallet could be monitored by the state.”

— Thomas Lohninger, epicenter.works.5

1.4 The Pseudonym Provider — A Central De-Anonymization Risk

The ARF introduces a “Pseudonym Provider” — a centralized entity that:

  1. verifies the user’s real identity,
  2. issues pseudonyms,
  3. maintains a mapping table,
  4. discloses the true identity upon request from law enforcement authorities.

This entity is inconsistent with the eIDAS Regulation’s stated intent. Art. 5a(4)(e) and Recital (11c) envision local pseudonym generation on the end device — not a centralized de-anonymization service. The Regulation does not explicitly prohibit a Pseudonym Provider, but the ARF’s interpretation takes a permissive reading where the Regulation envisioned a more restrictive one.

“The eID Wallet risks becoming a tool of surveillance and control rather than a tool that empowers users.”

— EDRi, “The eID Wallet still doesn’t deserve your full trust”, 10.03.2026.6

1.5 Article 45 — Mandatory Trust in State Certification Authorities

Article 45 of the eIDAS 2.0 Regulation requires browser manufacturers (Mozilla, Google, Apple) to recognize Qualified Website Authentication Certificates (QWACs) issued by EU member states. The final text (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183) includes a compromise: browsers may continue to apply their own security policies (including CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements) in addition to recognizing QWACs, provided they do not discriminate against QWACs compared to other certificates meeting the same standards. Whether this compromise is sufficient remains contested. Over 500 cybersecurity researchers warned in an open letter (November 2023):7

“The current proposal radically expands the ability of governments to surveil both their own citizens and residents across the EU by providing them with the technical means to intercept encrypted web traffic.”

— eidas-open-letter.org (over 500 signatories, including EFF, EDRi, Internet Society, Mozilla, Linux Foundation)

Partial Mitigation Through Certificate Transparency

The EU implementing acts (Commission Implementing Regulations (EU) 2024/3144 and 2024/3145, December 2024) require QWACs to be logged in publicly accessible Certificate Transparency (CT) logs. This significantly raises the bar for covert, large-scale TLS interception — a rogue certificate for gmail.com would appear in public logs and could be detected within hours. However, targeted, short-duration interception (e.g., of a specific journalist’s traffic) remains possible before CT monitors flag the anomaly. The structural concern — that Article 45 undermines browser vendors’ ability to enforce their own security standards — remains valid regardless of CT mitigations.31

Reference: Wazan, A.S. et al. (2024). “Article 45 of the eIDAS Directive Unveils the need to implement the X.509 4-cornered trust model for the WebPKI.” ARES ’24.8

1.6 Google’s Role in the German Prototype Development

In the SPRIND “Funke” competition, Google was admitted to the German wallet prototype development — despite documented data protection concerns within the jury. Google developed the “Android German EUDI Open Source Wallet Prototype” (GitHub: google/funke-eudi-wallet-prototype, since archived).

The involvement raises specific, documented concerns: (1) Google’s production identity stack (Android Credential Manager, Google Identity Services) remains proprietary and closed-source, creating a dependency on a platform whose business model is surveillance advertising; (2) the prototype uses Android’s StrongBox secure element for key storage, which is controlled by Google’s hardware security module — an opaque trust anchor for a European sovereignty infrastructure; (3) Google is simultaneously a Relying Party (as a platform that would verify age) and an infrastructure provider (as the wallet developer), creating a structural conflict of interest that the ARF does not address.52

Part 2: Societal and Fundamental Rights Implications ‹

2.1 Experiences with Real-Name Policies: The Case of South Korea

South Korea’s real-name policy (Klarnamenpflicht, 2004–2012) represents the most comprehensive natural experiment to date on the effects of mandatory online identification.

  • A government study by the Korea Communications Commission (KCC) found a decline in malicious comments of merely 0.9 percentage points (from 13.9% to 13.0%) — a figure that may fall within measurement error depending on the sampling methodology, which the KCC did not fully disclose — despite significant interference with fundamental rights.
  • Cho & Kwon (2015) showed that statutory identification requirements actually increased flaming: “Whereas policy-driven regulation does not reduce, and even increases, flaming, the voluntary approach significantly decreases it.”9
  • August 2011: The SK Communications breach (Nate/Cyworld) compromised registration data of 35 million accounts. The attack exploited a software vulnerability in SK Communications’ systems, but the real-name policy had required this data to exist in the first place — creating the attack surface that made the breach consequential.
  • August 2012: The Constitutional Court declared the system unconstitutional (Case No. 2010Hun-Ma47) — for violating freedom of expression and the right to informational self-determination (Recht auf informationelle Selbstbestimmung).

Limits of the Analogy

An important qualification: South Korea’s system was a full real-name policy requiring disclosure of the user’s civil identity for all online content. EUDI-based age verification is, at least in its stated design intent, a selective attribute attestation (“over 18: yes/no”). The analogy holds at the infrastructural level — both create a centralized identity verification system that can be extended — but differs at the disclosure level. The relevance of the South Korean case is strongest for the function creep argument (Section 2.4) and the data breach risk, less so for the direct chilling effects comparison.

Park, W.-I. & Greenleaf, G. (2012). “Korea Rolls Back ‘Real Name’ and ID Number Surveillance.” Privacy Laws & Business International Report, 119, 20–21.10

Leitner, J.M. (2015). “Anonymity, Privacy, and Expressive Equality.” Journal of Korean Law, 14(2), 167–212.11

2.2 Mandatory Identification as an Instrument of Political Persecution

The following examples do not suggest that the EU will necessarily follow the same path — the EU has stronger institutional safeguards (independent judiciary, Charter of Fundamental Rights, data protection authorities). They do, however, demonstrate that the technical infrastructure for identification, once built, can be repurposed under changed political conditions. The question is not whether the current government would abuse this infrastructure, but whether the architecture is robust against future political changes — including in light of democratic backsliding in EU member states such as Hungary and Poland.

The pattern is consistent across regimes: China’s real-name policy (Cybersecurity Law, Art. 24, 2017) enables not merely content censorship but the targeted suppression of collective action — King, Pan & Roberts (2013) demonstrated that it is not criticism of the state that triggers censorship, but any content with mobilization potential, regardless of tone.12 Turkey’s Law No. 7418 (2022) led to 4,590 investigations, 33 arrests, and 66 journalists affected — with nearly half of proceedings concerning reporting on the February 2023 earthquake. Russia’s blogger registration requirement (November 2024) enabled Roskomnadzor to classify content as “pro-government,” “anti-government,” or “apolitical” through systematic behavioral profiling. In each case, the identification infrastructure was introduced with stated objectives other than political repression.

2.3 Chilling Effects (Abschreckungswirkungen) — Empirical Evidence

Penney (2016): Wikipedia traffic to DHS-categorized “sensitive” articles dropped by approximately 27% after the Snowden revelations — from 3.0 million to 2.2 million views/month. The decline was persistent.13

Stoycheff (2016): Experimental study (n=255, convenience sample of US undergraduate students): Participants who were informed about surveillance and held a minority opinion expressed it significantly less frequently. Even surveillance supporters adjusted their behavior. The sample limits generalizability, but the mechanism is consistent with broader surveillance literature.14

Büchi, Festic & Latzer (2022): Perceived data surveillance leads to restricted digital communication — mediated by awareness, perceived intrusiveness, and resignation.15

Connection to Democratic Theory

Chilling effects matter not merely as individual privacy harms but as structural threats to democratic deliberation. Democratic legitimacy depends on the inclusion of diverse viewpoints, including dissenting and minority opinions (Habermas, 2006; Sunstein, 2017). Stoycheff’s (2016) finding that surveillance awareness suppresses minority opinion expression is an empirical instantiation of the “spiral of silence” (Noelle-Neumann, 1974) — intensified by the permanence and traceability of digital identity. Mandatory identification does not merely chill individual expression; it structurally degrades the epistemic conditions for democratic self-governance.32

Habermas, J. (2006). “Political Communication in Media Society.” Communication Theory, 16(4), 411–426. DOI: 10.1111/j.1468-2885.2006.00280.x.

Sunstein, C.R. (2017). #Republic: Divided Democracy in the Age of Social Media. Princeton University Press.

Applicability to Age Verification: A Necessary Qualification

The studies cited above document chilling effects under conditions of general surveillance perception (post-Snowden mass surveillance, government monitoring programs), not specifically under age verification. The transferability rests on a plausible but empirically untested mechanism: that awareness of identifiability — regardless of the stated purpose — alters behavior. This mechanism is consistent with the theoretical framework (Nissenbaum’s contextual integrity, Lyon’s surveillance culture), but a direct empirical test of chilling effects from age verification systems does not yet exist. The hypothesis is: mandatory age verification, by requiring identity attestation as a precondition for accessing public digital spaces, creates an identifiability awareness that produces chilling effects structurally similar to those documented in surveillance contexts — though likely attenuated in magnitude. This hypothesis awaits empirical testing.

2.4 Function Creep (Zweckentfremdung) — Recurring Patterns

Once identity infrastructure exists, behavioral profiling becomes technically trivial — the same data linkages that enable age verification can support scoring, classification, and surveillance of user behavior across contexts. The Dutch SyRI system illustrates this trajectory: it linked 17 government data categories (tax, social benefits, immigration) and was deployed exclusively in low-income, immigrant-majority neighborhoods before being declared incompatible with Art. 8 ECHR (Rechtbank Den Haag, 05.02.2020, ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878).

“Function creep is an imperceptibly transformative and therewith contestable change in a data-processing system’s proper activity.”

— Koops, B.-J. (2021). “The concept of function creep.” Law, Innovation and Technology, 13(1), 29–56.16

Function creep is not inevitable — but it is structurally favored by institutional incentives (bureaucratic expansion, political pressure during crises, exploitation of sunk costs). The burden of proof should lie with proponents of new identification infrastructure: What structural safeguards prevent the function creep that has regularly occurred in comparable systems?

System Original Purpose Documented Expansion Outcome
EU COVID Certificate (Regulation (EU) 2021/953) Cross-border travel Restaurants, sports, workplaces; in Italy, workplace access withdrawn for unvaccinated persons over 50. Note: The EU COVID Certificate Regulation expired on 30 June 2023, and the infrastructure was sunset — a positive precedent for time-limited function creep. The EUDI Wallet, however, is designed as permanent infrastructure without a sunset clause. Sunset June 2023 (positive precedent)
Schengen Information System Information exchange Supranational identification and investigation tool Expanded, operational
India’s Aadhaar Social benefits Bank accounts, SIM cards, taxes; biometric error rate 49% in Jharkhand17 Expanded despite documented harms
EU Interoperability Framework (Regulations 2019/817, 2019/818) Border/migration management Police access to EES, VIS, ETIAS, Eurodac via “hit/no-hit” mechanism Expanded, operational
EU CSAM Regulation (COM/2022/209, proposed) Detection of child sexual abuse material Client-side scanning of private messages; scope expanded from known CSAM to “unknown” material and grooming detection via AI classification60 Contested; partially rolled back by EP LIBE

A fair counterpoint: the EU COVID Certificate’s sunset in June 2023 demonstrates that function creep is not inevitable in democratic systems — time-limited infrastructure can be dismantled. The critical difference with the EUDI Wallet is structural: the COVID Certificate was designed as a temporary, crisis-specific instrument with a built-in expiration date. The EUDI Wallet is permanent general-purpose identity infrastructure with no sunset clause, mandated across all 27 member states. Once identity credentials are normalized for content access, the political cost of removing that requirement exceeds the cost of extending it to new use cases.

Blasi Casagran, C. (2021). “Fundamental Rights Implications of Interconnecting Migration and Policing Databases in the EU.” Human Rights Law Review, 21(2), 433–457.18

The EU CSAM Regulation: Function Creep in Real Time

The proposed EU Regulation on Child Sexual Abuse (COM/2022/209) — widely known as “Chat Control” — is the most instructive current example of function creep in the child protection domain. The original stated objective is the detection of known CSAM (child sexual abuse material) using perceptual hash matching. However, the proposal as introduced extends far beyond this: it includes detection of previously unknown CSAM and grooming detection via AI-based text classification of private messages — a qualitative expansion from matching known illegal images to surveilling the content of private communications.61

The institutional response has been unambiguous in its concern:

  • EDPS Opinion 4/2022: The European Data Protection Supervisor concluded that the proposal “would constitute a disproportionate interference with the fundamental rights” to privacy and data protection, specifically warning against the “general monitoring of the content of interpersonal communications.”62
  • German government position: Germany has consistently opposed the mandatory scanning provisions, with the Federal Government stating that “client-side scanning is incompatible with the confidentiality of communications” (Vertraulichkeit der Kommunikation). The Bundestag has issued formal objections.
  • European Parliament LIBE Committee: The Committee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs voted in November 2023 to exclude end-to-end encrypted communications from scanning obligations and to limit detection orders to known CSAM — effectively rejecting the AI-based grooming detection component.

The CSAM Regulation proposal demonstrates the precise mechanism this section documents: a legitimate child protection objective serves as the entry point for an infrastructure that, once built, expands beyond its original scope. The progression from “detect known illegal images” to “classify private text conversations using AI” occurred within a single legislative proposal — before the system was even deployed. This is function creep not as a gradual institutional drift but as a design choice embedded in the initial architecture.

The relevance to age verification is direct: the CSAM Regulation and the SPD’s age verification proposal share the same policy-ratchet logic. Child protection establishes the normative justification; a technical infrastructure is built; the infrastructure’s capabilities exceed its stated purpose; and the gap between capability and mandate narrows over time as new use cases emerge.63

2.5 Fundamental Rights Classification

CJEU, Digital Rights Ireland (C-293/12, C-594/12, 08.04.2014): The CJEU annulled the Data Retention Directive — due to indiscriminate, generalized collection of all persons without cause. A blanket identification requirement for all social media users — including adults — exhibits a structural parallel in scope: it indiscriminately captures the entire user base in order to enforce a norm that applies only to a subset (minors). The analogy has limits: Digital Rights Ireland concerned continuous mass retention of communications metadata over 6–24 months, not one-time attribute verification. The parallel lies in the indiscriminate scope, not in the nature or duration of data processing.

CJEU, Tele2 Sverige / Watson (C-203/15, C-698/15, 21.12.2016) and La Quadrature du Net (C-511/18, C-512/18, C-520/18, 06.10.2020): The CJEU held that national measures requiring general and indiscriminate retention of traffic and location data are incompatible with Art. 15(1) of the e-Privacy Directive read in light of Arts. 7, 8, 11, and 52(1) of the Charter. The Grand Chamber in La Quadrature du Net — now the controlling framework — refined this into a tiered system: general retention is permissible only for threats to national security; targeted retention for serious crime; and “quick freeze” orders for ordinary crime. The CJEU has to date recognized only the fight against serious crime and threats to national security as justifications for general, indiscriminate data measures. Whether child protection could reach this threshold has not been directly addressed, though the existing tiered framework suggests a high bar.33

CJEU, Opinion 1/15 (PNR Agreement, 26.07.2017): The CJEU required that even legitimate bulk data collection be strictly purpose-limited and accompanied by precise criteria limiting subsequent processing (paras. 187, 204–209). The analogy in scope is clear — mandatory age verification captures data from all users to protect a subset (minors) — though the volume and sensitivity of data differ: PNR involved 39 data elements per passenger, while a properly implemented ZKP-based age attestation would transmit a single boolean attribute (see Section 2b.4 for this qualification).34

ECtHR, S. and Marper v. United Kingdom [GC], Nos. 30562/04, 30566/04, 04.12.2008: Blanket retention of biometric data of unconvicted persons violated Art. 8 ECHR due to its “blanket and indiscriminate nature” (para. 125). This case is arguably more directly relevant than Art. 10 cases because mandatory identification interferes with informational privacy regardless of whether the user speaks.35

EDPB Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance (15 January 2025): Europe’s data protection authorities explicitly state that age verification mechanisms must comply with the data minimisation principle (Art. 5(1)(c) GDPR) and should not lead to “excessive processing of personal data of all users, including adults.” The EDPB distinguishes between age verification (precise age determination) and age assurance (reasonable confidence about age group), recommending the latter where possible. Importantly, the EDPB does not categorically oppose age verification: it acknowledges that verification may be necessary for services where access is legally restricted to adults (gambling, alcohol, pornography). This paper’s critique is directed at universal age verification for general social media access, not at age verification for specifically regulated content.36

BVerfGE 65, 1 — Census Ruling (Volkszahlungsurteil, 15.12.1983): Those who do not know what information about their behavior is being stored will preemptively adjust their behavior. This panoptic effect is incompatible with a free democratic order. A blanket identification requirement without specific cause (Anlassbezogenheit) raises significant constitutional concerns under this doctrine. The BVerfG has reinforced this principle in the context of automated data analysis: in its ruling of 16.02.2023 (1 BvR 1547/19), the court held that automated data analysis generating new insights constitutes an independent interference with fundamental rights beyond the original data collection, with a particularly aggravating effect where threat assessment is “conducted by machine in the sense of predictive policing.”

BVerfGE 120, 274 — IT Fundamental Right (IT-Grundrecht, 27.02.2008): The fundamental right to the confidentiality and integrity of information technology systems (Grundrecht auf Vertraulichkeit und Integrität informationstechnischer Systeme) was created to protect against covert state infiltration of IT systems (specifically, the secret installation of surveillance software). Its direct applicability to the EUDI Wallet — a voluntarily installed application — is doctrinally uncertain. However, if wallet adoption becomes effectively mandatory (through age verification requirements) and the wallet’s architecture permits state or provider access to transaction data, the IT-Grundrecht’s protective scope may be engaged. The more directly applicable constitutional basis is the right to informational self-determination (BVerfGE 65, 1, above).

UN Special Rapporteur David Kaye, A/HRC/29/32 (2015):

“Encryption and anonymity, separately or together, create a zone of privacy to protect opinion and belief.” (Para. 12)

“States should not restrict encryption and anonymity, which facilitate and often enable the rights to freedom of opinion and expression. Blanket prohibitions fail to be necessary and proportionate.” (Para. 60)

ECtHR, Standard Verlagsgesellschaft v. Austria (No. 3), No. 39378/15, 07.12.2021: Violation of Art. 10 ECHR because the Austrian legal framework lacked adequate procedural safeguards — specifically, no prior judicial review — for orders requiring disclosure of anonymous commenters’ identity. The ECtHR’s holding was primarily procedural (unmasking requires independent review), not a broad endorsement of absolute online anonymity, but it confirms that anonymity is a protected interest that requires procedural protections before it can be overridden.

Council of Europe, Declaration of 28.05.2003, Principle 7: “Member states should respect the will of users of the Internet not to disclose their identity.”

Art. 8 EU Charter — Data Protection as Autonomous Right: Art. 8 of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights protects personal data independently from Art. 7 (private life), requiring purpose limitation, a lawful basis, and independent oversight. The CJEU has increasingly treated Art. 8 as a self-standing right with its own requirements. Mandatory age verification must satisfy Art. 8’s specific demands — including purpose limitation and independent supervisory control — in addition to the broader proportionality test under Art. 52(1).

CJEU, Prokuratuur (C-746/18, 02.03.2021): The CJEU held that access to retained data requires prior review by a court or independent administrative authority. This is directly relevant to the Pseudonym Provider (Section 1.4): if the Provider can disclose real identity upon law enforcement request, Prokuratuur requires prior judicial authorization — a safeguard not specified in the ARF.

Digital Exclusion and Accessibility

Mandatory age verification via digital identity systems raises a distinct accessibility concern. Globally, an estimated 850 million people lack any form of legal identification (World Bank ID4D, 2024). Within the EU, populations disproportionately affected by digital exclusion include older adults, persons with disabilities, undocumented residents, and economically marginalized communities. The EU Accessibility Act (Directive (EU) 2019/882) requires that digital services be accessible to persons with disabilities — an obligation that extends to any age verification interface. The EDPB’s Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance explicitly notes that age verification mechanisms “should not lead to the exclusion of users from services” — a concern that applies with particular force to populations already at the margins of digital participation. The UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD, Art. 9) further requires that barriers to accessing information and communication technologies be identified and eliminated. A universal age verification mandate risks converting a digital inclusion gap into a digital exclusion wall.64

Note on the ECtHR Margin of Appreciation

Under ECHR law, states enjoy a margin of appreciation in determining how to protect competing rights, particularly in areas where there is no European consensus. Child protection on the internet is precisely such an area — different states are adopting dramatically different approaches (France: mandatory verification; UK: design codes; Australia: age verification legislation). The ECtHR would likely grant a wide margin of appreciation for measures aimed at child protection, which is a relevant consideration in assessing ECHR-based objections to age verification.

2.6 eIDAS 2.0: Internal Contradictions

The eIDAS 2.0 Regulation itself contains provisions that tension with mandatory wallet-based age verification:37

  • Art. 5a(1): “The use of European Digital Identity Wallets shall be voluntary.” If social media access is contingent on EUDI Wallet-based age verification, this effectively compels wallet adoption — converting a voluntary tool into a mandatory one.
  • Art. 5a(16): “The use of European Digital Identity Wallets shall not be a precondition for accessing public or private services.” Social media platforms are private services.
  • Art. 5b(3): Relying parties “shall not request users to provide any personal data beyond what is strictly necessary for the purpose of the online service.” Platforms requesting full identity credentials when age attestation alone suffices would violate this provision.
  • Art. 5a(4)(e) and Recital (11c): Pseudonyms should be “generated and used locally” by the wallet — directly contradicting the ARF’s centralized Pseudonym Provider (Section 1.4).

Part 2b: The Case For Age Verification — A Fair Assessment ‹

The preceding sections have documented substantial risks of mandatory age verification via EUDI Wallet. Intellectual honesty requires that the strongest arguments for age verification be presented with equal rigor. This section steel-mans the pro-verification position before identifying where it is contested. The aim is not to dismiss these arguments but to engage them seriously and locate the genuine points of disagreement.

2b.1 The Scale of Online Child Exploitation

The harms that age verification proponents seek to address are not hypothetical. The Internet Watch Foundation (IWF) confirmed 275,652 URLs containing child sexual abuse imagery in 2023 alone, with 93% being self-generated material — children coerced into producing images through direct social media contact.42 NCMEC CyberTipline reports rose from approximately 100,000 in 2010 to 36.2 million in 2023 (20.5 million in 2024 following a reporting methodology change); in 2024 the CyberTipline received nearly 63 million individual files. Financial sextortion reports to the CyberTipline tripled between 2021 and 2023, with one in six reports mentioning self-harm or suicide.

These harms occur through the basic functionality of user accounts and direct messaging — not through algorithmic amplification. A determined adult can contact a child if both have accounts on the same platform, regardless of how the recommendation algorithm is configured. This is the strongest argument against relying exclusively on design regulation: design changes cannot prevent grooming if both parties have access to the same platform.

Sources: IWF Annual Report 2023; Thorn, “Child Sexual Exploitation and Technology.”

The Displacement Problem: Encrypted Messaging

A further structural limitation of age verification deserves acknowledgment by its proponents: the displacement problem posed by end-to-end encrypted (E2EE) messaging. WhatsApp (~2 billion users globally), Signal, and iMessage encrypt communications such that the platform operator cannot inspect content. Age verification operates at the platform’s “front door” — controlling who may create an account. But once an adult and a child are connected on a platform, grooming can migrate to encrypted direct messages where no content-based intervention is possible, or to entirely separate E2EE services. This is not a hypothetical: the National Crime Agency (UK) and Europol have documented migration of CSAM distribution from open platforms to encrypted channels (Telegram, Matrix, peer-to-peer) following platform-level enforcement. Age-verifying the front door of Instagram does not affect communications on WhatsApp — both owned by Meta, yet subject to fundamentally different technical constraints.65

The displacement problem connects directly to the EU CSAM Regulation debate (Section 2.4): the proposed “Chat Control” regulation attempts to address this gap by mandating client-side scanning of encrypted messages — illustrating the policy-ratchet logic. Age verification that cannot reach encrypted communications creates pressure for the next intervention that can, leading to proposals that undermine encryption itself.

2b.2 The Mental Health Debate: Neither Settled Nor Dismissible

The scientific evidence on social media’s impact on youth mental health is genuinely contested. This analysis must be transparent about the state of the debate rather than selectively citing one side.

The alarm position (Haidt, 2024; Twenge, 2017): A synchronized international rise in adolescent anxiety, depression, and self-harm beginning around 2012–2013 coincides with smartphone and social media saturation. Haidt reports correlations of r = .15–.20 for girls specifically (figures from his popular book; peer-reviewed meta-analyses such as Hancock et al., 2022, report smaller effect sizes), and cites quasi-experimental evidence that high-speed internet rollout produced 90% increases in mental health diagnoses for girls in Spain. The US Surgeon General issued a formal advisory in 2023 stating there is “not enough evidence to determine that social media is sufficiently safe for children.”43

The skeptical position (Orben & Przybylski, 2019): Specification curve analysis across 355,358 adolescents found digital technology use explained at most 0.4% of variance in well-being — comparable to the effect of wearing glasses or eating potatoes. Their core methodological point: analytical flexibility allows researchers to produce dramatically different results from the same data.44

The middle ground (Valkenburg et al., 2022; Beyens et al., 2020): The differential susceptibility model shows that the average effect is near zero, but this masks enormous individual variation. Some adolescents are meaningfully harmed, some benefit, and most show no measurable effect. The field lacks consensus, and interpretation depends on one’s threshold for what constitutes a meaningful effect.45

The best available meta-analytic estimate: Hancock, Liu & French (2022) conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of 226 studies (n > 275,000 participants). The overall association between social media use and a combined measure of well-being was r = 0.01 (not statistically significant), but this aggregate masks meaningful variation across subdimensions: small positive associations with depression (r = .12, p < .01) and anxiety (r = .13, p < .01), and a stronger positive association with social well-being (r = .20, p < .01). The most accurate characterization: the relationship is small, dimension-dependent, and near zero in aggregate. This is the most methodologically rigorous aggregation of the evidence to date and should be positioned as the baseline against which both alarm and skeptical claims are evaluated.73

The honest assessment: Neither side is clearly right. Social media likely has a real but modest average effect that is substantially larger for specific subgroups (girls, pre-existing vulnerability, heavy users, younger adolescents). The mechanisms are more complex than simple dose-response. The APA’s 2023 advisory took a nuanced position: social media is “not inherently beneficial or harmful” but “poses risks” that depend on individual characteristics.

Post-2022 developments: Since the debate’s initial polarization, the evidence base has continued to evolve. Orben et al. (2024) replicated and extended the specification curve approach with a global dataset (n>400,000 adolescents across 35 countries), finding that the association between social media use and life satisfaction remains small and inconsistent across countries — undermining claims of a universal causal mechanism. Coyne et al. (2024, Child Development) found in a longitudinal panel study that social media use at baseline did not predict depression or anxiety two years later, after controlling for baseline mental health — the strongest evidence against a simple causal link. However, the US Surgeon General (2024) issued a call for warning labels on social media platforms, arguing that even small population-level effects translate into large absolute numbers given the scale of use. The field remains divided, but the methodological quality of skeptical research has strengthened since 2022.66

Haidt, J. (2024). The Anxious Generation. Penguin Press.

Orben, A. & Przybylski, A.K. (2019). Nature Human Behaviour, 3, 173–182. DOI: 10.1038/s41562-018-0506-1.

Valkenburg, P.M., Meier, A. & Beyens, I. (2022). Current Opinion in Psychology, 44, 58–68. DOI: 10.1016/j.copsyc.2021.08.017.

Beyens, I. et al. (2020). Scientific Reports, 10, 10763. DOI: 10.1038/s41598-020-67727-7.

Allcott, H. et al. (2020). American Economic Review, 110(3), 629–676. DOI: 10.1257/aer.20190658.

2b.3 Digital Identity Systems That Have Not Been Abused

The analysis in Part 2 draws heavily on negative precedents (South Korea, China, Turkey, Russia). Fairness requires acknowledging that not all digital identity systems have been misused:

Estonia’s e-ID has operated for over 20 years with ~99% population coverage and no documented case of systematic surveillance abuse. Citizens can see who accessed their data and when — a transparency mechanism that has led to prosecution of unauthorized access. The 2017 ROCA vulnerability (~800,000 affected cards) was discovered through proactive security research and resolved through certificate suspension and renewal, demonstrating the system’s self-correcting capacity.46

Norway’s BankID reaches ~95% of the adult population (~85% of total population including minors) and has been used for age verification (including at Vinmonopolet, the state alcohol monopoly) since 2024. No systematic privacy abuse has been documented in 20 years of operation. However, BankID is an online verification model where the identity provider (the bank) is involved in every authentication transaction, creating a centralized correlation point — structurally similar to the wallet provider tracking concern raised in Section 1.3. Academic research has identified protocol-level vulnerabilities and the system reveals full identity rather than enabling selective disclosure.47

India’s Aadhaar presents the most complex picture. On the positive side: 1.4 billion enrollments, bank account ownership rising from 35% to 80% of adults, and an estimated $33 billion saved through elimination of ghost beneficiaries (World Bank). On the negative side: 49% of beneficiaries in Jharkhand experiencing at least one biometric authentication failure during the study period (Dreze et al., 2017) — not a per-transaction failure rate, but still a damning figure for welfare delivery that excluded the most vulnerable populations, and multiple data breaches including one where database access was purchasable for ~$6.48

Further positive cases: Denmark’s NemID/MitID and Sweden’s BankID (~8 million active users) have been used for age-gated services for years without documented systematic surveillance abuse. These Nordic cases represent the most relevant comparison class for the EUDI proposal — democratic states with strong rule of law and mature digital identity infrastructure — and their largely positive track record must be weighed against the negative precedents cited in Part 2.

The key distinction: Estonia’s success rests on a distributed data architecture (X-Road connecting sector-specific registries, avoiding a single monolithic identity store), citizen-visible access logs, open-source infrastructure, and 28 years of incremental trust-building. The EUDI Wallet faces a hard 2026 deadline across 27 member states with heterogeneous institutional cultures — including states with documented democratic backsliding. The question is not whether digital identity can work (Estonia proves it can) but whether the specific architecture and political context of the EUDI Wallet provide adequate safeguards.

2b.4 Privacy-Preserving Age Verification: A Technically Feasible Alternative?

The analysis in Part 1 demonstrates that the current EUDI Wallet architecture fails to provide anonymity. But proponents argue that privacy-preserving age verification is technically feasible:

The CNIL “double anonymity” model (France): A three-party architecture where the age issuer knows the user’s identity but not which site they visit; an intermediary knows the site but not the user’s identity; and the platform knows only that the age requirement is met. Cryptographic blinding and organizational separation ensure no party holds both identity and usage data. The privacy guarantee comes from architectural role separation combined with blinded tokens, not from zero-knowledge proofs in the formal cryptographic sense.49

On-device age estimation: Neural networks running entirely on the user’s device estimate age from a selfie; the result (over/under threshold) is attested and sent to the platform. No image or biometric data leaves the device. However, this approach faces an “attestation trilemma”: it requires (a) on-device processing for privacy, (b) remote attestation for integrity (preventing the user from simply claiming to be over 18), and (c) no trusted third party for decentralization — at most two of these can be achieved simultaneously. In practice, attestation integrity depends on hardware trust anchors (Apple/Google attestation services), reintroducing a platform dependency. Recent systems (e.g., Yoti) report mean absolute error of ~1.5 years for ages 13–17, suggesting improving accuracy, though the trust anchor problem remains.

The critical question this analysis must address: If a technically well-implemented, privacy-preserving age verification system existed — one using ZKP-based selective disclosure with no linkability, no data retention, and no centralized de-anonymization — would the democratic and fundamental rights objections in Part 2 still hold?

The answer is: partially. Even a technically perfect system creates an infrastructure of verification that did not previously exist. The function creep risk (Section 2.4) applies to the infrastructure, not the specific implementation. A system built for age verification today can be extended to verify other attributes tomorrow — political affiliation, health status, immigration status — through the same institutional channels. Moreover, on-device age estimation still requires the norm that accessing public digital spaces requires proving something about oneself — a norm that, once established, is difficult to constrain. But the degree of concern is substantially lower for a well-designed ZKP system than for the current EUDI Wallet architecture. The analysis’s critique is strongest against the specific proposal (EUDI Wallet with signed credentials), not against the abstract concept of privacy-preserving age attestation.

2b.5 The Bootstrapping Problem of Design Regulation

Proponents of age verification raise a structural objection to this paper’s preferred alternative (design regulation): design regulation cannot protect children it cannot identify. The UK Children’s Code sets privacy defaults to “high” for users the platform knows are children. But a child who lies about their age at signup — claiming to be 18 — receives no AADC protections whatsoever. The Code does not require age verification; it assumes platforms already know which users are children.50

This is a genuine weakness in the design-regulation-only approach. The rebuttal must be honest: design regulation works best for systemic design features (disabling algorithmic amplification for all users, removing infinite scroll by default, requiring opt-in to personalized feeds) that protect everyone regardless of age. It works less well for age-differentiated protections (restricting DMs, hiding certain content categories) that require knowing the user’s age.

Existing Legal Obligations and Parental Rights

Two further pro-verification arguments deserve acknowledgment. First, the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD, 2018) already requires age verification for on-demand video services showing pornographic content. Platforms face legal liability for knowingly exposing minors to harmful content; without some form of age assurance, they cannot know who is a minor. This is not a hypothetical concern but an existing legal obligation. Second, the UN CRC General Comment No. 25 (2021, paras. 19–22) recognizes parents’ right and duty to guide children’s digital environment. Age verification can be seen as a tool enabling parental oversight — a consideration this paper’s emphasis on children’s agency (Interim section) should not obscure.

The optimal regulatory mix may therefore include some form of age assurance — but the proportionality principle demands that it be the least invasive form possible, and the current EUDI Wallet proposal does not meet that standard.51

The UK Online Safety Act as a “do both” test case: The UK’s Online Safety Act 2023 represents the most ambitious attempt to combine design regulation (the AADC) with age verification requirements (Part 5, §§11–12). Ofcom’s draft Codes of Practice (published March 2025) require age assurance for services likely to be accessed by children, alongside continued enforcement of design standards. Early implementation reveals the tensions this paper predicts: platforms have focused compliance resources on age-gating mechanisms rather than on the more costly systemic design changes the AADC requires — precisely the moral licensing dynamic described in the Interim section. The UK experience will provide the first large-scale empirical test of whether combining both approaches produces additive protection or substitution effects.67

Interim Consideration: Proportionality Analysis Under Art. 52(1) EU Charter ‹

Having presented both the risks (Part 2) and the strongest arguments for age verification (Part 2b), this section conducts a structured proportionality analysis under Art. 52(1) of the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights. Art. 52(1) requires that any limitation on fundamental rights must (1) pursue a legitimate aim recognized by the Union, (2) be suitable for achieving that aim, (3) be necessary — i.e., no less restrictive alternative achieves the same objective — and (4) be proportionate stricto sensu — i.e., the benefits must not be manifestly disproportionate to the costs. This four-step test structures the analysis that follows.

Step 1: Legitimate Aim — Conceded

The protection of minors from online harms is a legitimate aim recognized by the Union. Art. 24(2) of the EU Charter requires that “in all actions relating to children, whether taken by public authorities or private institutions, the child’s best interests must be a primary consideration.” This is a Treaty-level obligation. The documented harms are real: according to Meta’s own internal research (disclosed by Haugen, 2021; not independently peer-reviewed), among British teens who already experienced suicidal thoughts, 13% traced them to Instagram; 17% of affected teens reported worsened eating disorders; and 32% of teen girls who felt bad about their bodies reported feeling worse when using Instagram. The IWF confirmed 275,652 URLs containing child sexual abuse imagery in 2023 alone (Section 2b.1). This analysis does not contest the legitimacy of the aim.

Step 2: Suitability — Conceded with Reservations

The suitability test asks whether mandatory EUDI-based age verification is capable of achieving the stated objective. Under established CJEU doctrine, this is a low threshold: a measure is suitable if it is capable of contributing to the objective at all. The evidence suggests marginal but non-zero effectiveness:

  • South Korea (Section 2.1): The most comprehensive natural experiment yielded a decline in harmful comments of 0.9 percentage points (from 13.9% to 13.0%) — alongside 35 million compromised data records.
  • Australia (Online Safety Amendment Act 2024): VPN-based circumvention was documented within weeks of the announcement. The eSafety Commissioner’s technical trial found that no system achieved both high accuracy (>95%) and low friction simultaneously.68
  • France (Loi SREN, 2024): ARCOM has struggled to find a technically compliant solution meeting the Conseil constitutionnel’s strict privacy conditions — validating that the gap between privacy-preserving theory and deployment reality is substantial.69
  • UK (Online Safety Act 2023): Ofcom acknowledged that “no single age assurance technology” meets all requirements and adopted a technology-neutral approach, effectively conceding the implementation trilemma.70

The fundamental suitability problem is that age verification addresses access, not harm. The documented harms — algorithmic amplification, addictive design, engagement-maximizing recommendation systems — operate on users after they access the platform. An age-verified 16-year-old on the SPD’s “youth version” remains exposed to the same business model dynamics unless the platform’s design is separately regulated.

The suitability test is met. Age verification does restrict some minors’ access — 0.9% is marginal, but not zero, and the juridical threshold requires only that the measure can contribute to the objective. Conceding suitability, however, sharpens the analysis at the subsequent steps: the low marginal effectiveness becomes directly relevant to the necessity assessment (are less invasive alternatives equally or more effective?) and to proportionality stricto sensu (do the costs justify a marginal benefit?).

Step 3: Necessity — Fails

The necessity test is the most consequential: are there less invasive alternatives that achieve the same legitimate objective?

Design regulation as a less invasive alternative: The UK Age Appropriate Design Code (AADC) achieved 91 significant design changes on major platforms without requiring any identification infrastructure (Section 3.3). Instagram set all under-16 accounts to private; TikTok stopped nighttime push notifications for under-18s; YouTube disabled autoplay for children. These changes protect children systemically — regardless of whether the platform knows the precise age of each user — and they address the mechanisms of harm (algorithmic amplification, addictive features) rather than merely restricting access.

Privacy-preserving age assurance: The EDPB’s Statement 1/2025 on Age Assurance explicitly distinguishes between age verification (precise age determination via identity documents) and age assurance (reasonable confidence about age group). The EDPB recommends the latter where possible, noting that verification should be reserved for services where access is legally restricted to adults (gambling, pornography). Less invasive age assurance mechanisms include:36

  • The CNIL “double anonymity” model (Section 2b.4): architectural role separation ensuring no party holds both identity and usage data
  • On-device age estimation: neural networks running entirely on the user’s device, with no biometric data leaving the device
  • Privacy-preserving attribute attestation using zero-knowledge proofs (BBS signatures, as recommended by the cryptographers’ joint statement)

DSA enforcement: The Digital Services Act already prohibits profile-based advertising for known minors (Art. 28(2)), mandates algorithmic transparency (Art. 27), and bans dark patterns (Art. 25). Art. 28(3) explicitly states that platforms should not be incentivized to collect additional personal data to determine whether a user is a minor. The appropriate response to the DSA enforcement deficit is to close it — through dedicated funding, binding researcher access timelines, and redistribution of enforcement authority — not to introduce additional instruments subject to the same constraints.

Existing German law: Germany already requires age verification for specific categories of demonstrably harmful content (pornography, extreme violence) under the JMStV, §§ 4–5. The SPD proposal’s extension to all social media use for all content represents a qualitative leap from targeted content restriction to general identification — fundamentally altering the proportionality calculus.40

The necessity test fails because less invasive alternatives exist and are demonstrably effective. Universal EUDI-based age verification is not the least restrictive means of achieving the legitimate objective.

Step 4: Proportionality Stricto Sensu — Fails

Even if necessity were satisfied, the balance of costs and benefits fails the proportionality stricto sensu test:

Documented costs:

  • Mass identity infrastructure linking government credentials to content access for all users, including adults (Part 1)
  • Digital exclusion of populations without digital identity or technical capability (Section 2.5)
  • Contradiction with eIDAS 2.0’s own voluntariness principle, Art. 5a(1) (Section 2.6)

Projected risks (empirically grounded but not directly tested for age verification):

  • Chilling effects on expression, disproportionately affecting minority viewpoints (Section 2.3) — empirically predicted from surveillance contexts but untested for age verification specifically; the hypothesis is formulated in Section 2.3 and awaits direct empirical testing
  • Function creep risk inherent in permanent identity infrastructure without sunset clause (Section 2.4) — structurally favored by institutional incentives but not inevitable, as the COVID Certificate sunset (June 2023) demonstrates

Benefits: Marginal reduction in minors’ access to social media platforms, partially offset by circumvention (VPN, false credentials) and displacement to unregulated services. The benefits do not address the root causes of harm (algorithmic amplification, addictive design, surveillance advertising business model) — these persist regardless of age verification.

The disproportion is clear: a permanent mass identity infrastructure affecting all users in order to achieve a marginal child protection benefit that could be more effectively achieved through design regulation.

Regulatory Resources Are Finite

A further structural argument reinforces the proportionality finding. Regulatory bandwidth, enforcement capacity, and political capital are finite resources. Implementing and enforcing universal age verification consumes resources — technical standards development, platform compliance audits, DPA oversight, judicial review — that could be directed at design regulation and DSA enforcement, which address root causes rather than symptoms.

A secondary concern is the moral licensing effect: once platforms implement an age gate, they can argue that they have fulfilled their duty of care, weakening political pressure for structural reforms. This effect is documented in individual social psychology (Monin & Miller, 2001; Mazar & Zhong, 2010), and the cookie consent analogy is instructive: Nouwens et al. (2020) found that only 11.8% of cookie consent implementations meet GDPR minimum requirements — formal compliance displacing substantive regulation. A fair objection: this argument cuts both ways; design regulation could equally create licensing effects against age verification. Moreover, the transfer from individual psychology to institutional regulatory dynamics is asserted but not empirically established.38

Youth Voice and Agency

Research consistently shows that young people are not passive victims but active agents who develop strategies for navigating online risks (Livingstone & Third, 2017). The UN Committee on the Rights of the Child, General Comment No. 25 (2021), emphasizes that age verification measures “should not compromise children’s rights” to privacy (para. 69) and that “digital inclusion is essential for realizing the full range of children’s rights” (para. 12). Excluding under-14s from social media entirely — as the SPD proposes — restricts not only access but participation, expression, and social connection.39

Livingstone, S. & Third, A. (2017). “Children and young people’s rights in the digital age.” New Media & Society, 19(5), 657–670. DOI: 10.1177/1461444816686318.

Could One Not Do Both?

The “do both” response — implement design regulation and age verification — is theoretically appealing and cannot be dismissed categorically. The UK Online Safety Act 2023 represents the most ambitious attempt to combine both approaches; early implementation suggests platforms may focus compliance resources on age-gating rather than the more costly systemic design changes the AADC requires — but this observation is preliminary, as the OSA’s implementation is still in its early stages and definitive conclusions about substitution effects would be premature. The moral licensing concern — that formal compliance with age verification reduces political pressure for structural reforms — is plausible but rests on individual-level psychological findings (Monin & Miller, 2001) whose transfer to institutional regulatory dynamics is asserted, not empirically established.

“Do both” remains theoretically possible, and its viability depends on implementation details. The critical constraint is this: if both approaches are pursued simultaneously, age verification must take its least invasive form — privacy-preserving age assurance rather than identity-linked verification. The EUDI Wallet as currently specified (ARF v1.4/1.5) does not meet this requirement. A “do both” strategy that pairs design regulation with EUDI-based identification is not complementary but compounding — it adds the costs of identity infrastructure to those of design enforcement without evidence that the combination produces benefits exceeding either approach alone.

Part 3: Root Cause Analysis and Alternative Regulatory Approaches ‹

3.1 The Causal Chain

The business model of platforms is the primary structural driver of the harms that age verification is supposed to combat:

Surveillance advertising model (Zuboff, 2019)19 → Engagement maximization → Algorithmic amplification of outrage and polarization → Addictive design → Harms to all users, especially minors

Age verification intervenes at the endpoint (who is allowed to use the platform) rather than at the root cause (how the platform is designed and what business model drives it).

3.2 The Surveillance Advertising Model and Its Downstream Harms

The surveillance advertising model (Zuboff, 2019) generates its harms through a specific mechanism: engagement maximization drives algorithmic amplification of emotionally charged content, which produces measurable downstream effects across democratic integrity, public health, and epistemic quality. At the content level, Brady et al. (2017) found that each moral-emotional word in a social media post increased its diffusion by 20%, demonstrating how engagement algorithms systematically reward emotionally charged content. Milli et al. (2025) provide the strongest experimental evidence at the system level: engagement algorithms amplify partisan content (+0.24 SD), anger (+0.47 SD), and anxiety (+0.23 SD) — while users actually prefer chronological feeds.20 Meta generates 97.3% of its revenue from advertising; when users are given a choice, 54–96% reject personalized tracking (Apple ATT data; initial opt-in rates of 4% rose to ~46% globally over time). The model functions only under conditions of limited transparency and manipulative consent designs.23

According to Meta’s own internal research (disclosed by Frances Haugen, 2021; not independently peer-reviewed), Facebook’s Responsible AI team was structurally prevented from addressing algorithmic amplification of disinformation because it would have impacted growth metrics.21 The Cambridge Analytica scandal (2018) demonstrated the trajectory from data infrastructure to political weaponization: Facebook’s Graph API allowed a researcher’s app installed by ~270,000 users to harvest data from up to 87 million profiles (Facebook’s worst-case estimate), which was then used for psychographic voter targeting. This was not a system failure but the intended functionality of the platform’s data infrastructure, repurposed for political manipulation — resulting in a $5 billion FTC settlement.53

Democratic harms: The Myanmar genocide (2017–2018) demonstrated lethal consequences when engagement optimization operates without adequate moderation — the UN concluded Facebook played a “determining role” in spreading hate speech against the Rohingya.55 Users over 65 share nearly seven times more fake news than younger users (Guess et al., 2019), underscoring that platform harms are age-independent.54

Public health and epistemic harms: Loomba et al. (2021) found that exposure to online vaccine misinformation reduced vaccination intent by ~6 percentage points.56 Benkler, Faris & Roberts (2018) document how the attention economy systematically degrades epistemic quality — sensationalism outcompetes accuracy because the business model rewards engagement regardless of truth value.57

Addictive design: Social media replicates the variable reinforcement mechanisms documented in gambling design (Schull, 2012): infinite scroll, pull-to-refresh, autoplay, notification systems.22 The European Parliament’s Resolution on Addictive Design (12.12.2023, TA-9-2023-0459) called for regulatory measures against these mechanisms and a “digital right not to be disturbed.”

The structural conclusion: These harms are consequences of the surveillance advertising business model. They affect adults and minors alike. They cannot be addressed by regulating who accesses the platform — an age-verified Facebook would have been equally vulnerable to every harm documented above. They can only be addressed by regulating what the platform does. A fair qualification: the counterfactual — whether subscription-based platforms without engagement optimization would produce the same harms — has not been empirically tested. However, Milli et al.’s (2025) finding that users prefer chronological feeds over algorithmic recommendations suggests that engagement optimization is model-driven rather than user-driven, strengthening the inference that the business model, not the medium itself, is the primary causal factor.

3.3 Regulatory Approaches That Address Root Causes

Design Regulation Instead of Identity Regulation

This distinction maps onto Lessig’s (2006) four modalities of regulation: age verification is regulation by law (commanding identification); design regulation is regulation by architecture (changing what the system does).
