In regulatory theory terms, design codes represent a lower rung on the “enforcement pyramid” (Ayres & Braithwaite, 1992) — less coercive, more responsive, and demonstrably effective (as the UK AADC shows) before escalation to identity-based measures becomes necessary.41

Ayres, I. & Braithwaite, J. (1992). Responsive Regulation: Transcending the Deregulation Debate. Oxford University Press.

The fundamental difference: Age verification regulates who may use a platform. Design regulation changes what the platform does to all users. The former leaves the business model intact. The latter addresses the root cause.

“The systemic design choices at platform level are the root cause for these harms, affecting children and adults alike.” Age-based exclusion, EDRi argues, does not solve these root causes but merely delays exposure to harm.

— EDRi (2025). “Age Verification Gains Traction: The EU Risks Failing to Address the Root Causes of Online Harm.”24

UK Age Appropriate Design Code — Demonstrable Effectiveness

The British Children’s Code (15 standards, in effect since September 2021) regulates the design of the service, not the identity of the user. Result: 91 significant design changes on major platforms (5Rights Foundation / Children and Screens, 2024):

  • Instagram set all under-16 accounts to private (July 2021)
  • TikTok stopped nighttime push notifications for all under-18s
  • YouTube disabled video autoplay for children and introduced break/bedtime reminders
  • Google made SafeSearch the default for all children

ICO Interim Impact Review (November 2025): Improvements affect over 3 million children. The Code has already been adopted in Indonesia, Vermont, and Nebraska. A symmetric evidence standard requires noting that these 91 changes are platform-reported and have not been independently audited for substantive user impact — just as age verification’s measured 0.9% effectiveness in South Korea reflects actual outcomes rather than formal compliance. The comparative case for design regulation rests on plausibility and design logic, not on equivalent outcome evidence.

“Always says the digital world, not social media, and always says by design not don’t do this and don’t do that.”

— Baroness Beeban Kidron, 5Rights Foundation.

Honest limitations of design regulation: The AADC’s success should not obscure structural weaknesses in the design regulation model. First, GDPR Art. 25 (“data protection by design and by default”) has been in force since 2018, yet enforcement actions specifically targeting design failures remain rare — the EDPB has issued guidance but no binding decisions specifically on Art. 25 compliance. Second, the US COPPA (Children’s Online Privacy Protection Act, 1998) has demonstrably failed to prevent platforms from collecting children’s data — the FTC has imposed over $400 million in COPPA penalties since 2019, yet structural compliance remains poor. Third, the AADC’s claimed 91 platform design changes have not been independently audited for substantive impact: a platform changing a default setting may satisfy formal compliance without meaningfully altering user experience. These failures do not invalidate design regulation as an approach, but they demonstrate that design codes face the same enforcement gap this paper identifies for age verification — an honest assessment must acknowledge this symmetry.72

DSA — The Enforcement Reality

The Digital Services Act (Regulation (EU) 2022/2065) already contains a comprehensive toolkit:

  • Art. 25: Prohibition of deceptive or manipulative interface design (commonly referred to as “dark patterns”)
  • Art. 27: Algorithmic transparency — users must be able to modify recommendation parameters
  • Art. 28(2): Prohibition of profile-based advertising for known minors
  • Art. 34/35: Systemic risk assessments and mitigation measures for VLOPs
  • Art. 28(3): Platforms should not be incentivized to collect additional personal data in order to determine whether a user is a minor

The problem is less a regulatory deficit than an enforcement deficit. But this raises a critical question that the paper must address head-on: why have these tools not been effectively enforced?

Structural causes of the enforcement deficit:

  • Resource asymmetry: The European Commission’s DSA enforcement unit comprises approximately 100 staff for overseeing 19 designated VLOPs and VLOSEs. By comparison, Meta alone employs over 40,000 content moderation personnel and spends more on lobbying in Brussels (EUR 8 million in 2023) than the Commission’s entire DSA enforcement budget.
  • Technical opacity: DSA Art. 40 grants vetted researchers access to platform data, but platforms have systematically delayed and restricted access. As of March 2026, no independent researcher has received full algorithmic audit access under Art. 40(12). Without this access, systemic risk assessments (Art. 34) rely on platform self-reporting — a structural conflict of interest.
  • Political economy: The same governments proposing age verification have not allocated the resources necessary for DSA enforcement. Germany’s Digital Services Coordinator (Bundesnetzagentur) received EUR 15 million for DSA enforcement in 2025 — for a jurisdiction with 67 million internet users. This suggests that the push for age verification may, in part, reflect a preference for measures that shift costs to platforms and users rather than to the public budget.
  • Jurisdictional complexity: Ireland hosts the European headquarters of Meta, Google, TikTok, Apple, and Microsoft. The Irish DPC’s well-documented delays in GDPR enforcement (7+ years for some cases) illustrate how regulatory bottlenecks in a single member state can compromise EU-wide enforcement.

A critic could rightly object that this analysis is self-undermining: if the enforcement deficit is as structural as described, design regulation faces the same obstacles, and “better enforcement” is a wish rather than a policy proposal. This objection has force. The response is twofold: (1) design regulation has a lower enforcement burden than age verification — once a platform changes its default settings (as the UK AADC demonstrated), the change applies to all users without per-user verification; (2) enforcement capacity is demonstrably building, even if slowly. The EDPB’s binding decision on Meta’s legal basis for behavioral advertising (January 2023), which forced Meta to offer consent-based advertising in the EU, shows that existing tools can change platform behavior when applied. The European Commission’s first formal proceedings under the DSA (against X/Twitter in December 2023 and TikTok in February 2024) confirm this trajectory. The appropriate response is to address the structural causes: dedicated funding, binding researcher access timelines, sanctions for non-compliance with data access requests, and redistribution of enforcement authority away from single national bottlenecks — not to introduce additional instruments subject to the same constraints.58

Digital Markets Act — Addressing Market Power

The DMA (Regulation (EU) 2022/1925) addresses the monopoly structure: interoperability requirements for messaging (Art. 7), prohibition of cross-service data combination without consent (Art. 5(2)), data portability (Art. 6(9)). First fines: EUR 500 million (Apple) and EUR 200 million (Meta), April 2025.25

Restricting Surveillance Advertising

Norway banned Meta’s behavioral advertising in 2023; the EDPB extended the ban EU-wide. Context-based advertising (based on the content of the page, not the user’s profile) is demonstrably comparable or more efficient in cost-per-click metrics.26

Digital Fairness Act — The Next Step

Expected legislative proposal Q3 2026. Intended to address: manipulative interface design, addictive design, misleading influencer marketing, unfair personalization practices, special protection for minors. This would be design regulation — an approach that addresses causes rather than symptoms.

Conclusion ‹

This analysis set out to examine whether mandatory age verification via the EUDI Wallet is a proportionate response to the documented harms minors face on social media. Its core finding is that the specific architecture proposed — the EUDI Wallet as specified in the ARF v1.4/1.5, using signed credentials with static issuer signatures, a centralized Pseudonym Provider, and optional rather than mandatory unlinkable proofs — fails to deliver even the privacy-preserving age verification that proponents promise. The technology does not match the policy rhetoric.

Applying the four-step proportionality test under Art. 52(1) EU Charter, universal EUDI-based age verification fails on necessity and proportionality stricto sensu. Less invasive alternatives exist and are demonstrably effective: the UK AADC achieved 91 significant design changes without requiring any identification infrastructure; the EDPB recommends age assurance over age verification; privacy-preserving approaches (CNIL model, on-device estimation, ZKP-based attestation) can provide age confidence without linking government identity to content access. The marginal child protection benefit of universal age verification over these alternatives does not justify the creation of permanent mass identity infrastructure with documented chilling effects, function creep risks, and structural tensions with the eIDAS Regulation’s own voluntariness principle.

This paper is pro-better-technology, not anti-technology. It advocates for privacy-preserving age assurance mechanisms that meet the EDPB’s verification/assurance distinction — combined with robust design regulation that addresses the root causes of harm. The surveillance advertising business model, not the identity of users, drives algorithmic amplification, addictive design, and the downstream harms to democratic integrity, public health, and epistemic quality documented in Part 3. Regulating who accesses a platform without simultaneously addressing what the platform does misallocates regulatory priority.

The regulatory priority should be to close the DSA enforcement deficit, advance the Digital Fairness Act, and direct enforcement resources at platform design rather than user identity. Concretely: binding timelines for researcher access under DSA Art. 40, dedicated enforcement budgets commensurate with the scale of regulated platforms, redistribution of enforcement authority away from single national bottlenecks, and sanctions for platform non-compliance with data access requests. Adding new instruments subject to the same enforcement constraints — the same resource asymmetry, technical opacity, and jurisdictional bottlenecks documented in Section 3.3 — does not solve the institutional capacity problem. It compounds it.

Acknowledged Limitations

This analysis does not resolve every tension it identifies. Design regulation cannot prevent grooming through basic platform functionality (Section 2b.1) — a genuine gap that some form of age assurance may need to address. The enforcement deficit affects all regulatory instruments, including this paper’s preferred alternative. The proportionality analysis is conducted as a policy assessment, not a binding legal opinion — it lacks the specific safeguard analysis a court would require. And the paper’s conclusion aligns with the position of the surveillance advertising industry itself, which opposes age verification mandates — an alignment that should prompt reflection on whether the argument inadvertently serves the interests of the platforms it criticizes. The argument is not that no form of age assurance is ever justified, but that the specific proposal — mandatory EUDI Wallet-based identification for all social media users — fails the proportionality test against less invasive alternatives that address root causes.

On Ireland’s enforcement bottleneck: Brave (2021). “The Irish DPC: An Analysis of Delays in GDPR Enforcement.” On the resource asymmetry in DSA enforcement, see also: Access Now (2024). “Two Years of DSA: Implementation Under Scrutiny.” On successful enforcement: EDPB Binding Decision 01/2023 on Meta’s legal basis for behavioral advertising (adopted 13 April 2023), which resulted in Meta offering consent-based advertising in the EU. ↑
  59. U-Prove was developed by Stefan Brands (Credentica, acquired by Microsoft in 2008). U-Prove tokens operate on standard elliptic curves (including NIST P-256) without requiring pairings, offering single-show unlinkability. However, multi-show unlinkability requires additional mechanisms, and Microsoft has largely discontinued active development. See: Brands, S. (2000). Rethinking Public Key Infrastructures and Digital Certificates. MIT Press. On the comparison between BBS, CL, and U-Prove: Slamanig (2025), Section 3. ↑
  60. European Commission, Proposal for a Regulation laying down rules to prevent and combat child sexual abuse, COM/2022/209 final, 11 May 2022. The proposal requires “detection orders” that would oblige providers to scan communications for known CSAM (via perceptual hashing), new CSAM (via AI classifiers), and grooming (via text analysis). ↑
  61. On the scope expansion within the proposal: EDRi (2022). “EU’s CSA Regulation: A Threat to Encryption and Privacy.” The shift from hash-matching known material to AI-based classification of unknown content and text represents a qualitative change from pattern matching to content surveillance. See also: Abelson, H. et al. (2024). “Bugs in Our Pockets: The Risks of Client-Side Scanning.” Updated version, arXiv:2110.07450. ↑
  62. EDPS Opinion 4/2022 on the Proposal for a Regulation laying down rules to prevent and combat child sexual abuse, 29 July 2022. Available at: edps.europa.eu. ↑
  63. On the “policy ratchet” dynamic in surveillance legislation: Bernal, P. (2016). “Data gathering, surveillance and human rights: recasting the debate.” Journal of Cyber Policy, 1(2), 243–264. DOI: 10.1080/23738871.2016.1228990. ↑
  64. World Bank (2024). Identification for Development (ID4D) Global Dataset. On EU digital exclusion: Eurostat (2024), “Individuals’ level of digital skills” — 26% of EU adults have below basic digital skills; the proportion rises to 53% for persons aged 65–74. EU Accessibility Act: Directive (EU) 2019/882, Art. 4 (accessibility requirements for digital services). CRPD Art. 9 (accessibility). ↑
  65. National Crime Agency (UK) (2024). National Strategic Assessment of Serious and Organised Crime. On CSAM migration to encrypted platforms: Europol (2024). Internet Organised Crime Threat Assessment (IOCTA) 2024. On the structural incompatibility of content-based detection with E2EE: Mayer, J. (2019). “Content Moderation for End-to-End Encrypted Messaging.” Princeton CITP. ↑
  66. Orben, A. et al. (2024). “Social media and adolescent well-being: A systematic review of longitudinal studies.” Replicated and extended the specification curve approach globally. Coyne, S.M. et al. (2024). Child Development. Longitudinal panel study finding no prospective association between social media use and depression/anxiety after controlling for baseline mental health. US Surgeon General (2024). Call for warning labels on social media, building on the 2023 advisory. ↑
  67. UK Online Safety Act 2023, Part 5, §§11–12 (duties regarding children’s access). Ofcom (2025). Draft Children’s Access Assessments and Age Assurance Guidance. Published March 2025. On the interaction between AADC design standards and OSA age verification requirements, see: 5Rights Foundation (2025). “Age Assurance and Children’s Code: Complementary or Competing?” ↑
  68. Australian Online Safety Amendment (Social Media Minimum Age) Act 2024, assented 29 November 2024. eSafety Commissioner (2025). “Age Assurance Technology Trial: Interim Report.” On VPN circumvention: Reset Australia (2025). “Social Media Age Verification: Implementation Challenges.” ↑
  69. Loi n° 2024-449 du 21 mai 2024 visant à sécuriser et réguler l’espace numérique (SREN). Conseil constitutionnel, Décision n° 2024-866 DC du 17 mai 2024. ARCOM (2025). “Technical Specifications for Age Verification of Online Pornographic Content” — published February 2025, compliance deadline initially September 2025, subsequently delayed. ↑
  70. Ofcom (2025). “Protecting Children from Harms Online: Statement on Age Assurance.” The statement acknowledges the “implementation trilemma” and adopts a principle of technology neutrality, requiring services to use “highly effective age assurance” without mandating a specific technology. ↑
  71. Architecture Reference Framework (ARF), version 1.5, published December 2024. European Commission, DG CNECT. On the continuity of privacy deficiencies from v1.4 to v1.5, see: epicenter.works (2025). “ARF 1.5: Cosmetic Changes, Structural Persistence.” The cryptographers’ joint statement (June 2024) predates v1.5 but its core criticism — that the ARF does not mandate unlinkable proofs — remains valid. ↑
  72. On GDPR Art. 25 under-enforcement: Binns, R. & Veale, M. (2021). “Is that your final answer? On the tenuous link between transparency and accountability in machine learning.” On COPPA enforcement failures: FTC (2023). “COPPA: A Survey of Past and Present Enforcement Actions.” Total penalties exceeded $400 million since 2019, including $520 million against Epic Games (2022) and $245 million against Fortnite. On AADC audit gaps: Lievens, E. & Verdoodt, V. (2024). “Design Regulation for Children’s Rights: Implementation Challenges.” Computer Law & Security Review. ↑
  73. Hancock, J.T., Liu, S.X., Luo, M. & Mieczkowski, H. (2022). “Psychological Well-Being and Social Media Use: A Meta-Analysis of Associations between Social Media Use and Depression, Anxiety, Loneliness, Eudaimonic, Hedonic and Social Well-Being.” SSRN/Stanford Social Media Lab. 226 studies, 1,279 effect sizes, n > 275,000 participants. Overall effect r = 0.01 (n.s.); subdimensions: depression r = .12, anxiety r = .13, social well-being r = .20 (all p < .01). ↑
  74. SPD-Bundestagsfraktion (2026). “Kinder und Jugendliche in den sozialen Medien besser schützen.” Policy paper, February 2026. ↑
  75. EUDI Wallet rollout timeline: Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 requires member states to offer EUDI Wallets to all citizens by 2026. The infrastructure is mandated independently of any age verification use case. On the “sunk cost” dynamic in identity infrastructure: see Koops (2021), fn-16, on how existing infrastructure lowers the political cost of scope expansion. ↑

Revision History

In accordance with academic transparency standards, substantive changes to this analysis are documented below. Minor typographical corrections and formatting changes are not listed.

Date Version Description of Changes
2026-03-17 1.0 Initial publication. Single-file HTML document.
2026-03-18 2.0 Comprehensive revision: structural overhaul, peer review integration, gap analysis, proportionality framework, and fact-check corrections.
  • Structural changes: Part 2b (steel-man case for age verification) repositioned before Interim section. New Part 0 (“The SPD Proposal”) added as policy trigger. Interim section restructured around Art. 52(1) four-step proportionality test. Conclusion rewritten as five-paragraph synthesis. ~5,000 words cut (behavioral scoring, AI surveillance sections removed; Part 3 sections merged). Technical migration to Vite + Handlebars partials.
  • Cryptographic corrections: BBS+ terminology updated to BBS per IETF CFRG. Hardware curve claim corrected (BLS12-381, pairing computation bottleneck). U-Prove added as P-256-compatible alternative. CNIL model corrected from ZKP to cryptographic blinding. On-device attestation trilemma documented. ARF versions (1.4, 1.5) specified throughout.
  • Legal corrections: La Quadrature du Net (2020) added as controlling framework. Digital Rights Ireland analogy qualified. BVerfGE 120,274 doctrinal fit qualified. Art. 8 EU Charter and CJEU Prokuratuur added. ECtHR margin of appreciation noted. EDPB Statement 1/2025 integrated.
  • New content: EU CSAM Regulation (“Chat Control”) analysis. E2EE displacement problem. Real-world deployment data (Australia, France, UK). Digital exclusion / accessibility section. Post-2022 mental health update. Design regulation failures. UK OSA as “do both” test case. DSA enforcement deficit structural analysis. Cambridge Analytica case study.
  • Empirical corrections and qualifications: Wikipedia traffic decline corrected to ~27%. SK data breach causation clarified. Bail et al. (2018) recharacterized. YouTube radicalization claim marked as contested. Cambridge Analytica 87M qualified as worst-case estimate. Haidt correlations qualified as popular book figures. Aadhaar 49% clarified. South Korea analogy limits added. Chilling effects hypothesis explicitly formulated.
  • Fact-check corrections: Hancock et al. (2022) meta-analysis effect size corrected from erroneous d = −0.10 to r = 0.01 (n.s.) overall, with subdimension correlations (depression r = .12, anxiety r = .13, social well-being r = .20). Apple ATT rejection rate corrected from 90–96% to 54–96%. IWF self-generated CSAM corrected from 92% to 93%. NCMEC/Thorn statistics replaced with verified data. Meta internal research precision improved (Instagram statistics clarified as referring to teens with pre-existing issues).
  • Balance improvements: Art. 24 EU Charter given more weight. Proportionality analysis qualified as policy assessment. Moral licensing acknowledged as cutting both ways. Nordic positive cases added. Enforcement deficit self-undermining tension addressed. Paper explicitly characterized as critical policy analysis with argumentative thesis.
  • Footnotes: Reorganized from 61 to 75 total (additions, removals, renumbering). ~25 new reference entries across three new categories.
2026-03-18 2.1 Argumentative corrections: logical consistency, symmetric evidence standards, and qualification of unsupported claims.
  • Suitability test: Reclassified from “Contested” to “Conceded with Reservations” — juridically correct (low threshold met), strengthens credibility of necessity/proportionality arguments.
  • Proportionality costs: Differentiated into documented costs and projected risks. Chilling effects and function creep qualified as empirically grounded but untested for age verification specifically.
  • “Do both” objection: UK OSA evidence qualified as preliminary. Moral licensing transfer to institutional dynamics acknowledged as unestablished. “Do both” treated as theoretically possible, contingent on least-invasive AV form.
  • Root cause claim: “Root cause” replaced with “primary structural driver.” Counterfactual limitation acknowledged; Milli et al. evidence integrated.
  • Evidence symmetry: AADC’s 91 changes explicitly qualified as platform-reported and unaudited, paralleling SK’s measured outcomes.
  • Conclusion: “Category error” replaced with “misallocates regulatory priority” — avoids false dichotomy.
  • Function creep table: Added “Outcome” column showing non-deterministic trajectories (sunset, expansion, judicial rollback).
2026-03-18 2.2 Peer review fixes and Alpine.js interactive features.
  • Critical fix: Duplicate id="fnref-36" across part2.html and interim.html resolved.
  • Accessibility: ARIA attributes added (TOC nav, footnotes section). Heading hierarchy h1→h3 skip fixed in method-box. Back-link for fnref-1b added.
  • CSS: Dead code removed (padding override). Inline styles extracted to CSS classes (header, footer, revision-history). color-scheme meta tag added. Smooth scrolling enabled.
  • Build: base: './' added for portable deployment. Part 2b subsection numbering (2b.1–2b.5) aligned with Part 1/2/3 convention.
  • Alpine.js integration: Scroll-spy TOC with active section highlighting. Footnote tooltips (hover/click inline preview). Collapsible sections for all main parts. Dark/light/system theme toggle with localStorage persistence. Back-to-top button. Mobile TOC sidebar with slide-in navigation.

This document was compiled from academic sources, court rulings, and official EU documents. March 2026.
Generated with the assistance of Claude (Anthropic). All sources independently verified by the author.
